item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: my hat is definitely off to you, sir. that's really impressive. thanks for this. :-) it was Jewish enough that i was pretty giddy — especially because it's one of the best reads I've enjoyed on this site! I have immense respect for goyim who know their Jewish stuff. rad as hell. and you know the word goyische. be still my beating heart!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, goyische author with a deep interest in the 1st Century Middle East.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this by explicitly searching for jewish SCP articles /tales/ characters. I know we have a golem (or two?), and the oil jug, a couple of tales with Jewish characters, but that's all I've really found so far so I decided to search it out more carefully.

while the author has seemed hesitant to describe it as specifically Jewish, it certainly had a strong enough flavor to it (especially with that heartbreaking supplement — mentioning the shoah and everything [I know plenty of goyim who don't even know what that word means], in that letter that poor man's daughter will never read; this author really knows their stuff, if they're a goy color me super impressed!) that I was certainly not disappointed that this came up as a result. this is such a detailed and intricate piece. talk about a finesse play. this may be one of the most dense reads I've encountered here, esp. with the density being of a mythological nature and not a scientific one. I definitely dig it. a hearty +1.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The first image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as no source found indicates it has been released under Creative Commons. The second image comes from here and is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm afraid I don't have the background in religious studies or understanding of the Jewish faith necessary to truly enjoy this article. I also don't have the inclination to do the amount of background reading necessary to fully understand it. Those facts combined with the slightly confusing containment procedures (from what I understood in the Containment Procedures section there was a fairly definitive ritual requiring very specific participants and then in the Description it is stated that the Foundation is making it up as they go based on what they observed the original participant doing) leads me to reluctantly downvote. It is a fantastically well thought out and researched scip I just really didn't enjoy it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is interesting because it's both excellent and fails to deliver the message.

From other sources on the wiki and off it, I understand that this article is supposed to tie into the author's Homo Decensus mini-canon, connected series, or what have you, and, reading this article, there is absolutely nothing at all that suggests that. No name-drops, no references, no crosslinks.

But it's still an excellent article, so +1 for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, I wish I could upvote this. Clearly a lot of work was put into it, but it’s so dense and hard to understand. The ‘Easter eggs’ the author inserted are more like required reading to understand most of what’s going on. It’s so hard to parse and requires actual research. I don’t know, maybe I’m just to dense to fully comprehend what’s going on.

From my understanding it’s the coffin of the abrahamic God, who is dead or dying, and for some reason a ritual needs to be enacted to keep the coffin closed. Am I right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the lore and the effort put into this skip enough to yield a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest, I'm not sure I would have liked this one as much as I did if I hadn't read others before it….however, I did, so it got me thinking and trying to figure exactly what it was.

My first thought was the entity/god/whathaveyou known as Prometheus, because of the radiation (the whole stole the fire of the Gods and give it to man thing) and the mention in the interview that "Thy don't remember very long" (the Olympians were notoriously fickle and they could go from wanting to kill you to simply forgetting about you if something else got their attention) , and the ritual was simply a "modernization" of the original. After all, if you take religious ritual as just a form of "ritual magic", it could be theorized that all of this type of religious rituals can be traced back to a "root form", so the particular religion used as a framework wouldn't matter as long as it held true to that "root form". (Much like if a cake recipe calls for flour, you can use any type you want. Even though each will be slightly different, you are still going to end up with a cake at the end.)

Then I thought to take it simply as stated, that it was Abrahamic in "origin". Taking into account that simply being in the presence of an angel or demon isn't ever described anywhere as killing anyone as far as I can recall offhand, that pretty much left "God", who it IS mentioned would kill a person were they ever to be in "His presence" directly. That in turn left me wondering about SCP-343, which is commonly referred to as "God" (and I've read the comments on it…it seems the jury is still out on that). But that left a bit of a paradox, because if 343 is God, then 1348 can't be, right? Then it suddenly hit me that both of them could be, and not through any form of omnipresence…but by the simple fact that modern Abrahamic faiths "have it wrong". Taking the bible as an example, 1348 could very well be the "God of the Old Testament", while 343 was the "God of the New Testament" which, considering the apparent personality of 343 would nicely explain the personality shift of "God" between the Old and New Testaments….the books are actually talking about two different beings, both of which "are God", and simply do not realize it.

Then I read your explanation….and I was disappointed that I was wrong on both counts. Paradoxically enough though, that actually made me like it more as, since I have a tendency to figure out the endings of books before I get to them, I always enjoy it when I run across something were I get juuust enough of it "right" that I'm close, but it is still different then how I thought it was going to turn out.

Very well done.

item.title: Re: Downvote
item.content:encoded:
That's not an unfair criticism.

SCPs are written from an in-universe perspective, which means that it often makes sense to leave out facts which would be obvious to the people who are supposed to be reading the document, but which are relatively obscure for people who aren't. The problem for this one is that there really aren't paramilitary atheist theologians supervising God's sarcophagus, so I had to do one of two things:

(1) Break format in order to deliver a lot of exposition about things which readers aren't going to be interested in, which I already do to a fair extent, or;

(2) Commit to the obscurity of the idea which I'm trying to express, and write it as an ARG-like puzzle box for people to play with.

This doesn't need me to be particularly clever, though — this is all stuff which I'm interested in because I'm really interested in Middle Eastern minority religions. Not everybody is, and that's more than fine. And it's easy to look things up, then hide them from people, or make them implicit in other things; it's necessarily much harder to find things that are hidden or implicit. So it's not "I'm much smarter than you," it's "hey, I stashed away a whole bunch of Easter eggs, and here's where I put them."

If you didn't like it, you didn't like it. Which is fine: it's written to provoke investigation and reading extra stuff, and if you're not actually interested in doing that (which is fine, because it's written based on a pretty niche subject) then it's just going to be frustrating.

item.title: Downvote
item.content:encoded:
This article is for me a "floor" when it comes to articles that I have read on the site. I have yet to post one of my own so I'm not attacking the process of writing but the article itself failed to draw me in, is in places incomprehensible, and then the comments from the author, here in discussion, allude to the fact that he believes himself to be very clever and that we readers have yet to "get it" because understanding his world will be worth it.

It was a learning experience for me because I believe that I have read longer, more difficult-to-digest articles here, ones I enjoyed a great deal, but this one just left me feeling frustrated, both with the product and the author's comments.

I'm going to try the others and see if I have an "a ha" moment because in general I prefer difficult, research heavy work but I am not hopeful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this one, and its easily one of my favorite posts on this site. Its both smart and esoteric, and its that esotericism that prevents this from being a "oh its the Jewish/Christian god but in the magical realist style of an SCP" and makes into its own world.

More than that, and what I think is shown through all of your entries, Ophite, is that you do a great job of showing off the humanity in how the Foundation does its business day-to-day. With this one, you show the complete disdain the foundation would have for anything that would demand a religious ritual, and how gung-ho they are to change the containment procedures to something both less demanding, and more "real world" and scientific. In other articles, you make great use of the Containment procedures section to show the Foundation's culture, and thats one of those things that sets your entries above the rest.

All i gotta say is, keep on keepin on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fucking stellar as always, ophite. This is sublime.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I though that the time of religion and folklore based entities as SCP entries passed long ago, and I usually expect them to be written not originally, but here it is done exceptionally well. It is also cool to see SCPs that reference real world objects, texts and events (which is nicely done in Ophite's works). When done well, this usually gives me a nice sense of conspiracy that Foundation and GoI's maintain, and also makes the SCP seem more real. For similar reasons, I like SCPs that make you research, because that gives extra "oh wow" effect. Y-chromosomal Aaron reference was a very clever move.

Also, though I consider myself agnostic, I am really fond of symbolism. I enjoyed how this ties not just into common Abrahamic faiths, but into myth of Prometheus, and maybe Gnosticism (though into last one in a kind of a weird reverse, demiurge-is-good fashion). I also enjoyed the scepticism that Foundation shows towards this object, despite all the religious references, brushing off probable divine epiphanies as memetic effects, because of their they want to believe into science-explained world until the end of it.

tl;dr: Impressive research, a lot of detail, and I like the style of writing. Upvoted. I think this could be my new favorite SCP, or at least my most favorite Keter-Class SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I'm posting spoilers on this thread, a note on why I chose South Semitic rather than Central Semitic: I needed something which correlated well with the origins of humanity, and which provided an explanation for the fact that more PACG sites haven't been found. This site in Syria is actually one of the furthest-east of their settlements, and the center of their culture was in what is now the Sahara, but which (when they were still around) was the temperate shores of a warm, shallow inland sea.

The early Homo sapiens in Ethiopia are their descendants, and Amharic, Ge'ez and a bunch of extinct Yemeni languages are direct descendants of PACG languages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You spent a while Googling, so I may as well repost the spoilers from this article's sister-SCP, 1427:

(1) There have been small populations of decensus on Earth since roughly two million years ago. They naturally clustered in small populations around the various inner sanctums of the Elohim, who had apparently uplifted or created them.

Human development toward sapiens is the result of interbreeding breeding between Homo sapiens decensus and Homo erectus in an attempt to raise the naturally miserable fertility rate of decensus — there were never more than a few tens of thousands of them on Earth at the same time, and you look at the archaeological record in 1427, you'll see a lot of decensus infant remains in their midden. Insofar as they had a relationship with decensus at all, many early sapiens were unskilled laborers around decensus settlements. But there were a lot of wild sapiens around, which were mostly ignored by decensus, and didn' t compete with them for resources (largely because there were so few left.)

Decensus technology was heavily based on the Elohim they served: they used their gods as energy sources, among other things. Devices like SCP-1427 were multifunction defensive perimeters: they were used to keep wild populations of sapiens away and servant populations of sapiens servile. They powered themselves by drawing energy off of nearby radioactivity; originally, an Elohim sanctum, but they can technically pull power off of any available source within a very large range. Right now, the 1427 obelisk is drawing power from North Korea's nuclear program — which is why their bombs keep mysteriously fizzling — but in the past, it powered itself up from a Russian nuclear power plant, a nuclear bomb, and Chernobyl NPP (eventually resulting in the meltdown).

The reasons the Germans could never get it to work is that they didn't have a suitable power source within range. Conversely, if 1427 were moved out of the range of something it could draw power from, it would shut down. But the Foundation hasn't discovered that yet, mostly because it can only hire unimaginative fascist drones to work on studying it.

(2) It is very unclear what the Elohim actually are: they are something like gods, and are of extraordinary but undefined power, but have very short attention spans and very little perspective on the world as humans see it. They seem to be more concerned with lineages than individuals, and more concerned with patterns than events. When the inhabitant of 1348 confused Dr. Kahn for his father, this was a reflection of its strange myopia about the world: it did not know that Kahn considered himself to be a different entity than his ancestors.

SCP-1348 is the last remaining sanctum for a member of the Elohim. The entity inside, Yehom, seems to have betrayed its brethren in order to save Homo sapiens, and was grievously wounded in some undefined way in defending humanity. When the beryllium bronze sheath lifts, the other Elohim can remember that it exists, and that puts it in danger. None of the Abrahamic religions' apocalypses can occur until the rest of the Elohim murder Yehom. (Presumably, then, the Elohim would turn on humanity and end them as well.)

The rest of the 1348-like inner sanctums were destroyed by sapiens populations. The rectangular divot in the Tamarranset Ossuary is, in fact, the remains of an Elohim inner sanctum like the one which Yehom inhabits. This appears not to have killed the Elohim which once lived there.

(3) SCP-1348-1-E is a different sort of servitor of the Elohim; it was the last of its type, and is now extinct. They may have been purpose-built from PACG infants, or may have been a different sort of being entirely. They survived entirely on the deadly neutron radiation emitted by the Elohim, in the same way that PACG technology was powered, and had only vestigial digestive tracts. So when the wounded seraph was removed from the Sanctum, it died of malnutrition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My oh my is this clever, though I think it might be too subtle\obscure for those without knowledge of Jewish\Semitic tradition, beliefs and phrases to decipher, even with a lot of wiki-trawling.

Would upvote ten times if I could, this is an exemplary piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For those interested, a minor tidbit of relatively obscure and hard-to-find information that might defeat people's wiki-trawling efforts:

+ What is Herev
- This is Herev

Herev (Hebrew: "Sword") Battalion is an infantry battalion within the Israeli Defense Force composed entirely of male recruits drawn from the Druze population. Why the Foundation would need well-trained Druze infantrymen for this ritual is not for me to reveal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also liked the article :P But I originally read it back when I was archive binging and didn't want to leave a thousand article long upvote list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Counter up-voted.

Ironically, I don't think that's a very good reason to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not a particularly good reason to downvote - in my opinion at least. Counter up-voted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's wrong with religion-themed articles?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find this over religious. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dearly want to upvote this. It's very well written, and you obviously spend a lot of time on this.

The thing that is preventing me from voting is my fault, not yours.

Quite simply put, I can't figure out what happens if the ritual isn't performed. I know that the information is in here, because this article wouldn't be so highly rated if it wasn't. What I'm asking is for you to point me to where this information resides.

Thanks!

EDIT: Found it, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure if I missed something but is there a clue that the three XK scenarios correspond to the Abrahamic doomsdays?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(1) Before 2006, the rituals were performed by the seraph who later starved to death in Foundation custody. We don't know much about the culture which made the Holy of Holies, but the archaeological report in SCP-1427 describes another of their sites.

(2) If the ritual isn't performed, then the veil will open. This isn't a Keter-level threat itself, but is an obligatory precursor to three different end-of-the-world scenarios. (The apocalypses of the three major Abrahamic religions.)

(3) The radiation poisoning isn't particularly dangerous. The SCP is Keter because allowing the veil to open for too long is a precursor to an XK-class event, and keeping the SCP from being destroyed requires a great deal of day-to-day intervention, as well as tolerating a memetic agent.
Congratulations!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My questions are not religious based but story based.
1. Has there ever been a time the rituals where not preformed (before 2006?)
2. What will happen if every one just leaves the site? The discussion alludes to world destruction but the description doesn't state what will happen.
3. Is it Keter because of radiation poisoning? Most other Keter SCP's have reoccurring run-ins with escapes or chances to harm large populations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just figured I'd post here to say thanks for making me look up the Druze — this is fascinating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my new favourite article, and I think you're my new favourite writer, ophite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For those of you still waiting for spoilers, they're on my personnel file:

http://www.scp-wiki.net/ophite-s-personnel-file

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with wishun here. I simply don't have the time to try digging up everything, and I too understand just enough of the references that I have an idea what you're getting at, without really understanding it.

Even if just a private message to me, can you spell it out?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Believe it or not, but what pushed me over the edge of joining, or just continuing to lurk and read, was this SCP. Why? Because I think I figured it out, and I won't be happy until I do.

+ Spoiler?
- Maybe?

This isn't so much a god, or Gods, as it is us looking at the entirety of existence all at once, is it? They forget because the human mind can't hold that much information, and the 'Angels' sole purpose is to -hide this- from the eyes of those who can't handle it. And the 'Watchers' spoken of…those are just the other people, doing the ritual, aren't they? The pain experienced is for the person looking into everything at once. It's not sentient, atleast, not in the sense we'd understand. This is the focal point of existence. If I'm right, anyway.

Edit- Something else just hit me. Did you intentionally pick 1348 because it adds up to 7?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hilarious. But — actually — the three XK-class scenarios are the respective eschatons of the major Judeo-Christian religions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Speaking of easter eggs, I like to think that "scenarios XK-734, XK-918, and XK-337" aren't just arbitrary numeric XK designations, but are actually related to their respective SCPs.

In which case, full retraction of the veil would cause the hair of all Foundation personnel to become violently self-aware, allowing anomalous storks to finally kidnap the baby (for being terrible) and take it back to the baby mill, where it would be processed into the deadliest cotton and milk you can possibly imagine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that's fair. The idea here (and the fact that it's so demanding) came out of wanting to do something sort of ARG-ish with a SCP. This is obviously a tremendously demanding read, and intentionally so, but that's sort of conceptually baked into it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this article, in spite of the fact that it may be the most demanding read on the site.
I got just enough of the cultural/religious references to realize how many I missed, which gives me the idea that I won't really udnerstand what's going on here until I do a lot of wikipedia reading. The sheer density you've managed to achieve without sacrificing tone or internal consistency is impressive. But let's be honest here, I'm not going to embark on a major research project to ferret out your punchline. And I don't think I'm alone in that.

I guess what I'm saying is, well-done, but most people are never going to understand what you're getting at because we are too lazy.
Can't you just tell us? If not in the article (IMO, it's better left un-said there), then how about spelling it out here in the discussion? Not with more hints and pointing out the easter eggs, either. We're too lazy for google, remember?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely adore this SCP. Definitely one of the best ones I've read. Upvoted. I'll be reading and researching all the easter eggs in this for a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, so, I've received half a dozen requests for spoilers, and put them out there. I'll repost them here in the form of questions:

(1) What people belong to CMH-6? What does the director's last name imply?
(2) What happened between Israel and Syria at the time the SCP was found?
(3) What are the humanoid's ten limbs? What does that imply about what it is?
(4) Which SCP is blacked out in the containment procedures?
(5) What language is the ritual in? What does that imply about the blacked-out SCP?
(6) Who are the Druze and Mandeans? What does Islam believe them to be? How did they avoid persecution?
(7) Why did the entity beyond the veil mistake Dr. Kahn for his father? Why did the celebrant claim that the entity was Ben Kahn's uncle?
(8) What is the veil there for? Who are the 'others' that soon forget about the entity's presence?
(9) Why are the ritual protocols (and 228-MELECHAH) named the way they are? What does that imply about their nature?
(9) What is 'Herev'? Why is it a potential source of recruits?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Though you'd think, if that were the case, it would be able to hold the veil in place on its own. Why the ritual?

Unless of course the point of the ritual has been completely misunderstood. It doesn't keep the veil in place, it lifts it through some permutation of prayer and waiting. So everyone's actually going, "Please, share your greatness with us," and it's sitting in there, holding onto the veil with all its might, going, "No, fuck you, stop doing that!" while struggling against the power of the prayer. Because, whatever it is, its nature is to respond to such supplications or somesuch.

That's probably completely implausible given what's here, though but dammit I like this game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand the references. At all. That being said, I'm not a religious person in the slightest and have no interest in researching so deeply into it…so I wouldn't be expected to know. This is still utterly fantastic though.
Plus all the ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is truly wonderful. As a speaker of both fluent Hebrew and limited Arabic, I appreciated the names you choose for the procedures and the use of an actual event as the catalyst. Though I don't understand all of the article, it makes me want to understand it, which is the important part. I doubt it is in my ability to write something this great, though I definitely plan to try. Kudos, sir, kudos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to upvote this article even more now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a panentheistic deity, isn't it?

When without the veil, it is literally everywhere and everything - drowning in water, floating in space, burning in flames, killing, dying, going insane. And it hurts.

So it created the veil, and as long as the veil is in place it is shielded from the rest of the word, maybe only partially, but shielded.

That explains O's ramblings about the lion and the uncle - the thing inside the veil is literally the lion, and the uncle, and you, and everyone else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly have no idea. My initial thought goes to The Lion King…but any time I try to think more into it, I just get more confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this took some work to slog through, but gorram hell I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's been a few tales and such, that indicate that 343 is, in fact, the being we know of as "God", as in the Abrahamic deity also referred to as YHWH.

There's also indications that, that aside, GOD isn't quite the way we think He is.

And there's also indications that a good many of the SCPs are not His creations, and in fact don't even come from a level of reality where He holds sway, and some not only don't even acknowledge him but He can't even perceive them in turn.

THIS is why I think the Foundation would try to contain 343: Either he's an incredibly powerful being that just so happens to be standing in for God… OR everything that something like 1/3 of the world's population currently holds faith in would be instantly, irrevocably proven wrong.

Edit: Forgot to add; of course, on this site the only canon is what's explicitly written out in the SCP files themselves, and what's in your own head. So basically, feel free to believe what you will that doesn't make you anger-bleed from the eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Or he's just a babbling lunatic.

This is another one of those, like the 7-kilometer arm, that doesn't need to be explained to be enjoyed. There's enough base for the weirdness to get a good sense of what's going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think maybe I need to footnote some of the interesting bits here. What's meant to be going on in that log is that the poor, addled celebrant had looked into the central region, and saw the Platonic essence of whatever was inside. He's now having difficulty explaining to anyone else what precisely it was he saw, except by half-assed metaphor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's somewhere between free-association and "everything is connected, man".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing says 343 is God, you can't take a religious view against something that quite possibly isn't religious at all. I do agree however that the article could be updated to fit with the times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's also where I stated why I don't think 343 is a bad article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Presuming there is an omnipotent deity in the world where SCPs exist, that deity is incomprehensibly malign. The problem of theodicy is "solved," in the real world, by blaming evil on human free will. But where intentional evil exists in the absence of human will, that inevitably makes evil (including, for instance, 110-Montauk) God's fault.

Not to mention that the containment procedures are 'give him whatever he wants' and the rest of the article is just raw, unalloyed lolfoundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's gone into at length on the discussion pages of 343 itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is it bad?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
343 isn't offensive, except as an SCP.

it's bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think offensive is the issue Ophite has with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see why, it's no more offensive than Able or 231.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, 343 is a bad idea which should have been deleted a long time ago. IMHO.
